text.title: Error Message Examples and Antipatterns
text.summary: Concrete good and bad error messages showing the three-part framework applied, plus antipatterns that cause abandonment or confusion.

example.note: Payment error (Stripe pattern) — clear what, context why, two recovery actions
example.code:
Your card was declined
Your card number is incorrect. Please check the number and try again.
[Try a different card] [Contact bank]

example.note: 404 page (GitHub pattern) — acknowledges the problem, provides navigation back
example.code:
This is not the web page you are looking for.
The page you requested doesn't exist. You may have typed the address incorrectly or the page may have moved.
[Go to GitHub.com] [Search GitHub]

example.note: Progressive password validation — real-time, specific, actionable
example.code: Password must contain: ✓ At least 8 characters ✗ One uppercase letter (add one) ✗ One number (add one)

example.note: Network error — identifies cause, offers retry and fallback
example.code:
Cannot connect to server. Please check your internet connection and try again.
[Retry] [Work Offline]

antipattern.description: Error codes as the entire message: "Error 403" or "ECONNREFUSED"
antipattern.reason: Error codes carry zero actionable information for non-technical users. They increase anxiety and support tickets without helping recovery.

antipattern.description: Generic messages: "Something went wrong" or "An error occurred"
antipattern.reason: Tells the user nothing about what failed, why, or what to do. Erodes trust by suggesting the product does not know what happened.

antipattern.description: Blame language: "You entered invalid data" or "Your request was malformed"
antipattern.reason: Shifts responsibility to the user even when the error is partially system-caused. Damages trust and increases frustration. Prefer neutral phrasing: "This field needs a valid email address."

antipattern.description: Dead-end errors: error message with only an [OK] button and no recovery path
antipattern.reason: Forces the user to figure out recovery independently. Every error must have at minimum one recovery action — retry, go back, or contact support.

antipattern.description: Payment-specific: "Your card is fraudulent" or "Suspicious transaction detected"
antipattern.reason: Legally and commercially dangerous. Use neutral phrasing: "Your card was not accepted. Please try a different card or contact your bank."

example.code:
You don't have permission to view this page. Contact your administrator for access.
[Request Access]
This email address is missing the @ symbol. Example: name@company.com
